sn47.19:1.1: This may be modern Sambalpur in Odisha, near the bay of Bengal on India’s east coast. This Sutta is the earliest description of Indian pole acrobatics or mallakambha. Further east lies Chandraketugarh in West Bengal, where the earliest physical evidence for mallakambha has been found. One pot from around 200 BCE shows, in detailed and graceful style, three acrobats using a T-shaped pole. One man kneels, holding the pole in his hands with great strength and balance, while a man and a woman perform acrobatics on opposite bars of the T. A crowd of women and men look on in amazement.
sn47.19:1.3: While the terms are not easy to interpret, it appears that it was a practice of the “corpse-workers” (caṇḍāla), the most despised caste (see SN 3.21:2.2 and note). Perhaps it originated as a tribal ritual or sport. Further references to this practice are found in DN 1:1.13.2 (see note there) and Ja 498, which is set near the opposite coast in Ujjeni.
sn47.19:1.4: Medakathālikā (“frying pan”) was a woman, which agrees with the depiction on the Chandraketugarh pottery.
sn47.19:1.8: The text implies that both acrobats were up the pole. Perhaps the plural is just a figure of speech, but in the pottery depiction there are indeed two suspended acrobats. I think the pole is planted on the ground, with the master half-way up, and Medakathālikā above him.
sn47.19:2.1: The text is a little unclear, but it seems that Medakathālikā was pointing out that by looking after oneself we are also looking after the other. It is remarkable how the Buddha is adopting the wisdom of a woman from such a despised caste.
